topics.title: Date & Time API (java.time)
topics.description: Java 8 introduced java.time (JSR-310) to replace the broken java.util.Date and Calendar. The key insight: immutable value objects (LocalDate, LocalTime, LocalDateTime, ZonedDateTime, Instant) that model date/time concepts precisely. Duration measures time between instants; Period measures calendar days/months/years. DateTimeFormatter handles parsing and formatting without thread-safety issues.
topics.story: The old Date class was a disaster — it stored milliseconds since epoch but had methods like getYear() that returned year-1900. Calendar was mutable and not thread-safe. Joda-Time (a third-party library) proved the right API design, and Java 8 adopted its approach as java.time. Now dates are like money amounts — immutable values you can add, subtract, and compare without mutating the original.
topics.analogy: LocalDate = a date on a wall calendar (no time, no timezone). LocalTime = a clock face (no date, no timezone). LocalDateTime = calendar + clock (still no timezone). ZonedDateTime = a calendar + clock + timezone label on the wall. Instant = a specific tick of the universal atomic clock (UTC offset). Duration = a stopwatch reading. Period = 'three months and five days' on a calendar.
topics.memoryAnchor: LocalDate.now(), LocalDate.of(2024,1,15). LocalDateTime.now(). ZonedDateTime.now(ZoneId.of('Asia/Kolkata')). Instant.now() — epoch-based. Duration.between(start,end) — for time. Period.between(date1,date2) — for dates. DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern('dd-MM-yyyy'). parse() vs format(). All immutable — plus/minus returns new object.
topics.firstPrinciples: java.util.Date mixes instant (absolute moment) with local date concepts, has deprecated mutating methods, and is not thread-safe. java.time cleanly separates: local (no timezone), zoned (with timezone), and instant (UTC epoch). Immutability makes them safe to share across threads.
topics.bruteForce: java.util.Date + SimpleDateFormat: not thread-safe (SimpleDateFormat has mutable state). Calendar.add() mutates in place. Date comparison with before()/after() works but verbose. Timezone handling is error-prone. Pre-8 code often stored dates as strings or long timestamps.
topics.optimizedApproach: java.time: LocalDate today = LocalDate.now(); LocalDate nextWeek = today.plusDays(7); long daysBetween = ChronoUnit.DAYS.between(today, nextWeek); DateTimeFormatter fmt = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern('dd-MMM-yyyy'); String s = today.format(fmt); LocalDate parsed = LocalDate.parse('15-Jan-2024', fmt);
topics.whenToUse: LocalDate: birthdays, calendar events, no time needed. LocalDateTime: log timestamps in same timezone. ZonedDateTime: user-facing times that cross timezones. Instant: storing in DB (convert to/from epoch). Duration: measuring elapsed time in tests/profiling. Period: 'subscription expires in 30 days'. For DB: store as TIMESTAMP/DATE; Spring Data JPA maps LocalDate/LocalDateTime automatically.

examples.title: LocalDate, LocalDateTime, Period & Duration
examples.explanation: java.time types are immutable — plus/minus return new instances. Period is calendar-aware (months have different days). Duration is precise (always seconds + nanos). DateTimeFormatter is thread-safe unlike SimpleDateFormat. ZonedDateTime handles DST transitions correctly.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Data JPA: @Column with LocalDate/LocalDateTime auto-mapped to DATE/TIMESTAMP. REST APIs: ISO-8601 format (DateTimeFormatter.ISO_DATE_TIME). Subscription expiry: LocalDate.now().plusDays(30). Audit fields: @CreatedDate Instant. Scheduling: next Monday = LocalDate.now().with(TemporalAdjusters.next(DayOfWeek.MONDAY)).

examples.title: Temporal Adjusters & Business Day Logic
examples.explanation: TemporalAdjusters allow complex calendar calculations without mutable state. Built-in adjusters cover common needs (firstDayOfMonth, nextOrSame(DayOfWeek)). Custom adjusters implement the functional interface TemporalAdjuster. This pattern is used for SLA calculation, billing cycle computation, and scheduling.
examples.realWorldUse: SLA deadline calculation (add N business days). Monthly billing cycles (first of next month). Expiry date calculation skipping weekends. Bank settlement dates. Reminder scheduling (next Monday after event).

problems.title: Age Calculator
problems.description: Write `calculateAge(String dob)` that accepts a date string in format 'yyyy-MM-dd' and returns the age in years as of today.
problems.testCases: provide a fixed past date and verify output is positive integer

problems.title: Convert IST to UTC
problems.description: Given a datetime string in format 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm' representing IST (Asia/Kolkata), return the equivalent UTC time as a string in the same format.
problems.hint1: Parse into LocalDateTime, then create ZonedDateTime at IST zone.
problems.hint3: Format back to string with the same DateTimeFormatter.

problems.title: Days Until Next Birthday
problems.description: Given a birthday as 'MM-dd' (e.g. '06-15'), calculate how many days until the next occurrence of that birthday from today. If today IS the birthday, return 0.
problems.hint1: Build the birthday in the current year with LocalDate.of(year, month, day).
problems.hint2: If birthday this year has already passed, check next year.
